one_line_takeaway: Over-generating GAN synthetic samples (WGAN best) for a rare NIDS attack class lifts Botnet detection on CIC-IDS2017 from F1 0.60 to 0.90, though the headline gain is partly measured on data derived from the same originals it is tested against.
key_finding: Augmenting the rare Botnet class with GAN samples substantially improves detection of the real Botnet traffic, and WGAN/Vanilla GAN outperform CTGAN. Best result: training with 99x WGAN-generated Botnet samples raises detection of the original 1956 Botnet samples to precision 1.00, recall 0.82, F1 0.90 (from baseline 0.87/0.46/0.60). Gains show diminishing returns (the jump from baseline to 4x is largest; 49x->99x adds little). CTGAN, despite being more sophisticated and tabular-specialized, produced lower-fidelity samples (lower cosine similarity, divergent cumulative sums) and weaker IDS gains, which the authors attribute to their segmentation producing near-constant columns that suit the simpler GANs. Crucially, performance on the seven non-Botnet classes stays stable (variations within ~4%), indicating the augmentation did not degrade other classes.
core_contribution: The paper proposes using GAN-generated synthetic attack samples to counter data scarcity/class imbalance in ML-based network intrusion detection, evaluated on CIC-IDS2017. Its own novel contributions (distinguished from related work) are threefold: (1) applying three distinct GAN variants (Vanilla GAN, WGAN, and Conditional Tabular GAN/CTGAN) to over-generate samples for a specific under-represented attack class (Botnet, 1956 instances); (2) explicitly validating the fidelity of the generated samples against the originals using three closeness measures (cosine similarity, cumulative feature sums, and an ML-classifier discriminability test) before using them; and (3) a scaling study that augments the training set with 4x, 49x, and 99x the original Botnet volume and measures how IDS detection performance responds. The augmented-then-retrained Random Forest IDS is tested on the ORIGINAL 1956 Botnet samples, where the best configuration (WGAN 99x) lifts Botnet detection from the unaugmented baseline of precision 0.87 / recall 0.46 / F1 0.60 to precision 1.00 / recall 0.82 / F1 0.90.
threat_model: Defensive NIDS setting: an RF-based multiclass classifier must detect and categorize malicious network flows (DoS, DDoS, Probe, Brute Force, Web Attack, Botnet, Infiltration) amid overwhelmingly benign traffic. The concrete problem is class imbalance and sample scarcity for rare attack classes (Botnet has 1956 instances, Infiltration only 36, vs. 2.27M benign), which biases the detector toward the majority class and yields poor recall on rare attacks. GANs are used here purely as a DEFENSIVE data-augmentation tool to improve the detector; the paper does NOT propose a GAN-based evasion attack (adversarial evasion GANs like IDSGAN and polymorphic-DDoS GANs are surveyed only in Related Work, not contributed here).
limitations: Only a single attack class (Botnet) is actually enhanced; Infiltration (the other weak class) is excluded for lack of data, so the method's generality to genuinely tiny classes is untested. The strongest reported numbers (Table 6, all metrics ~1.00) come from testing on held-out GENERATED data, which is close to circular; the meaningful evaluation is Table 7 on original samples. A leakage concern remains: the GAN samples are generated from segments of the same 1956 Botnet instances that later form the evaluation set, so improved recall on those exact originals may partly reflect the generators memorizing/interpolating them rather than true generalization to unseen Botnet traffic. Comparisons to Keserwani et al. (1.00/0.60/0.75) and Lee et al. (0.86/0.53/0.66) are taken from those papers rather than re-run under identical conditions, so the claimed 'new benchmark' is not a controlled head-to-head. Evaluation is confined to one dataset (CIC-IDS2017) and one classifier family (RF/DT); no cross-dataset or temporal generalization is shown. The Botnet segmentation is a manual, dataset-specific heuristic.

datasets_used: CIC-IDS2017 (78 features; cleaned then regrouped into 8 classes: Benign 2,271,320; DoS 251,723; Probe 158,804; DDoS 128,025; Brute Force 13,832; Web Attack 2,180; Botnet 1,956; Infiltration 36)
quantitative_results: Overall baseline RF accuracy: 0.9972 (chi2 top-32 features, 8:2 split)
Baseline Botnet detection (no augmentation): precision 0.87, recall 0.46, F1 0.60
Baseline Infiltration detection: precision 1.00, recall 0.67, F1 0.80
Best augmented Botnet on ORIGINAL 1956 samples (WGAN 99x): precision 1.00, recall 0.82, F1 0.90 (approx +13% precision, +35% recall, +30% F1 over baseline)
On generated held-out Botnet (Table 6), WGAN/Vanilla GAN at 49x and 99x reach 1.00/1.00/1.00; CTGAN 4x only 0.99/0.68/0.81
Cosine similarity of generated vs original (8 features): Vanilla GAN and WGAN ~0.76-0.9999, consistently higher than CTGAN (~0.55-0.9997)
ML-fidelity test (train on GAN+benign, test on original+benign): CTGAN 0.98/0.94/0.96; Vanilla GAN 0.99/0.92/0.95; WGAN 0.99/0.96/0.98
Non-Botnet class performance stable within ~4% after augmentation (Table 8, 99x WGAN)
baselines_compared: Own unaugmented RF IDS baseline on CIC-IDS2017 (Botnet 0.87/0.46/0.60) — the paper's primary internal reference
Keserwani et al. [57] GWO-PSO-RF feature selection: Botnet precision 1.00, recall 0.60, F1 0.75 (cited from their paper, not re-run)
Lee et al. [18] GAN generating 10,000 Botnet samples + RF: Botnet precision 0.86, recall 0.53, F1 0.66 (cited from their paper, not re-run)
Internal cross-GAN comparison: Vanilla GAN vs WGAN vs CTGAN; and RF vs Decision Tree for the fidelity check
